channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Trump’s China Trip: 3 Possible Deliverables
item.description: The United States and China need to lower expectations about Trump’s trip and focus on delivering a few concrete outcomes.

item.title: China’s New Five-Year Plan Prioritizes Robotics. The World Should Pay Attention.
item.description: Beijing is embarking on a "whole-of-nation push" to achieve permanent dominance in physical AI technologies.

item.title: Matthew Kroenig and Trita Parsi Debate the War in Iran
item.description: Will the campaign advance U.S. interests in the region or set them back?

item.title: Iran’s Strait of Hormuz Toolkit: Drones, Missiles, and Mines
item.description: A breakdown of the tools Iran has to maintain pressure on Trump.

item.title: Iran’s New Supreme Leader Vows to Continue Blocking the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Tehran escalates Gulf strikes and warns of future attacks on U.S. bases in the region.

item.title: How the New Gulf War Is Impacting Mongolia
item.description: Countries like Mongolia, despite being distant from the conflict, are already feelings its impact on their economic activities, not to mention how the war disrupts future possibilities.

item.title: Trump’s Last-Gasp Foreign Policy
item.description: Ill-considered overreach in Iran and elsewhere signals imminent decline.

item.title: Trump’s Efforts to Defuse the Oil Spike Aren’t Working
item.description: Big reserve releases, promises of escorts, and insurance can’t convince the market that the crisis will be over soon.

item.title: A Film of Unimaginable Horror and Unrelenting Humanity
item.description: ‘The Voice of Hind Rajab’ is an indictment of the international system that has failed Gaza.

item.title: Deterring China: The Growing Japan–Philippines Security Partnership
item.description: The growing partnership between Japan and the Philippines is not an actual alliance – but it could affect the balance of power in the South China Sea.

item.title: China’s High-Tech Narrative Cannot Solve Its Deflation Problem
item.description: For all the emphasis on developing advanced technologies, China’s present growth model still depends far more on low-cost manufactured exports.

item.title: South Korea’s Conservative Party Tanks Amid Warring Factions’ Fight for Legitimacy
item.description: The PPP ruined its local elections prospects by purging Han Dong-hoon, its former leader.

item.title: How to (Not) Be a Pacific Power
item.description: Washington has yet to recognize that mobility is an essential part of regional security.

item.title: How wars are adding hours to your flights
item.description: Our analysis shows which routes are getting longer

item.title: How China’s AI-Powered Robots Could Reshape the Global Order
item.description: China is moving from AI in the cloud to “embodied intelligence” with physical, real-world applications.

item.title: How to Electrify the Global South
item.description: Rather than rely on incremental efforts or one-size-fits-all models, countries hoping to achieve rapid progress on electrification must integrate distributed and centralized systems, make the most of AI-enabled tools, and send credible signals that attract private capital. India and Nigeria have shown how this can be done.

item.title: India’s Supreme Court Grants Bail to Veteran Kashmiri Separatist Shabir Ahmed Shah
item.description: The justices cited prolonged pre-trial detention exceeding six years, procedural irregularities in the trial process, and the slow pace of witness examination.

item.title: China is wrestling with a novel phenomenon: inherited wealth
item.description: Even as the economy slows and opportunity narrows, a lucky few receive big windfalls

item.title: Haiti needs order first, then elections
item.description: Voters must be able to turn out without risking death

item.title: How to teach Donald Trump a Latin lesson
item.description: By alienating Hispanics, he has given Democrats an open goal

item.title: China’s hereditary elite is taking shape
item.description: The Communist Party is afraid to tax inherited wealth

item.title: Islamists woo Bangladesh with everything but Islam
item.description: They are a hit on campus, too

item.title: Taiwan’s bid to export drones free of Chinese parts is taking off
item.description: The island’s unmanned aerial vehicles are already in use on Ukraine’s front line

item.title: Nepal’s new prime minister is a 35-year-old former rapper
item.description: Voters have given Balendra Shah an unprecedented mandate and enormous power

item.title: Seoul’s housing market is a huge political and economic headache
item.description: Lee Jae Myung is staking his presidency on fixing it

item.title: China’s nationalist spy thriller has few girls and lots of government
item.description: “Main melody” films are in a rut

item.title: Hong Kong’s property market has turned
item.description: That’s not good news for everyone

item.title: Some of China’s officials are becoming social-media stars
item.description: It may help them get promotions

item.title: America’s blame-Israel lobby
item.description: If the Iran war goes wrong, MAGA will want a culprit who is not named Donald Trump

item.title: Two very different states take aim at soaring hospital prices
item.description: But are price controls the answer?

item.title: Business
The weekly cartoon
Politics
A popular German Green wins a surprise victory
item.description: Cem Özdemir becomes the first Turkish-German to run a state

item.title: In Paris’s mayoral race, it’s drivers against cyclists
item.description: A decade of greening leaves the capital less congested but more divided

item.title: As war rages, Turkey‘s strongman puts the opposition on trial
item.description: Chaos in the Middle East makes President Erdogan indispensable to the West

item.title: Kenya’s ailing sugar sector is a test case for reform
item.description: Low productivity and political corruption bedevil the whole economy

item.title: What data reveal about the war’s progress
item.description: We analyse 1,615 distinct attacks, and more besides

item.title: Iran’s praetorian guard may emerge from the war diminished but undefeated
item.description: The regime’s enforcers will struggle to rebuild their capabilities and their deterrence

item.title: Who is Iran’s new leader?
item.description: No one knows if he will last

item.title: Why investors won’t know what to make of AI for a while
item.description: Markets always struggle to price technological revolutions

item.title: In praise of grunt work
item.description: AI promises to eliminate drudgery. Careful what you wish for

item.title: Altman, Amodei and Musk fight dirty for the biggest prize in business
item.description: Never before has so much money been sought from investors in a single year

item.title: Brazilian cinema is having its moment
item.description: Thank growing competition and a more internationally minded Academy awards

item.title: The Iran War’s Muddled Endgame
item.description: While Donald Trump has yet to articulate clear goals for the war in Iran, he would prefer to limit the energy shock and avoid a prolonged military engagement. But Israel does not share his reservations, and Iran views the current conflict as existential, so putting the genie back in the bottle will be no easy feat.

item.title: The Iran Crisis Is Fueling South Korea’s Drive for Nuclear Energy
item.description: Amid the Middle East conflict, South Korea is determined to find new pathways to strengthen localized, “autonomous” energy solutions by targeting civil nuclear and renewable energy.

item.title: India Is Not the 4th Biggest Economy in the World. But Does That Really Matter?
item.description: The nationalist narrative that dominated public discourse over the past year had been overstating the scale of India’s economic progress. Of particular concern is uneven and inequitable growth.

item.title: The War That Cracked the Postwar Order Illusion
item.description: Conflict in Ukraine has shattered the myths sustaining the Western coalition.

item.title: The American Narrative About a Revisionist China Is Upside-Down
item.description: The United States has become the dangerous revisionist power it long warned us that China would be.

item.title: Infrastructure Investment Is the Key to China’s Growth
item.description: China’s government has now set its growth target for 2026 at 4.5-5%. With China’s GDP deflator still in negative territory and ample policy space for more expansionary fiscal and monetary policy, this is achievable, but it will require boosting both consumption and investment spending.

item.title: Regional Crises Are Dealing a Heavy Blow to Afghanistan’s Fragile Economy
item.description: For a country whose economy remains deeply dependent on regional trade routes, migration, and external assistance, instability on multiple fronts can quickly translate into economic crisis.

item.title: The Iran War Is Now Impacting AUKUS
item.description: The HMS Anson’s abrupt departure from Australia shows the limits of Britain’s stretched fleet. That will have major implications for AUKUS.

item.title: Beijing Is Legalizing the Assimilation of Tibetans and Other Ethnic Minorities
item.description: China is amending its laws to weaken ethnic identities, including Tibetan language and culture.

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: How baseball created 20th-century America
item.description: Jon Fasman, our senior culture correspondent, explores the making of a national pastime in an age of immigration and industrialisation

item.title: Moving Up, Locked In: The Hidden Costs of ASEAN’s Semiconductor Push
item.description: As the region moves up the chip value chain – toward AI development, advanced logic, and high-bandwidth memory – U.S. regulatory pressures increase.

item.title: Scotland Is Pointing the Way to a New Economy
item.description: As more people worldwide confront economic failures and climate disruptions, systemic change will become increasingly more attractive. Scotland’s new Community Wealth Building Bill can serve as an example of a policy framework that promotes broad-based ownership, collective decision-making, and the reconfiguration of economic power.

item.title: America’s war on Iran may bring Bahrain to its knees
item.description: The tiny, indebted kingdom is seeing its main sources of revenue dry up

item.title: The damage to the world economy from the Iran war will be severe, but uneven
item.description: It has already caused the biggest energy supply shock in history

item.title: The Growing Cyber Risk to Supply Chains
item.description: Many corporate leaders regard cybersecurity as an internal IT problem that can be delegated and forgotten. But as AI and automation reshape global supply chains, cyber readiness has become an operational capability, similar to quality or safety, with the goal being continuity in the face of disruption.

item.title: NATO and the IP4: Potential and Limitations
item.description: The growing attention to NATO’s Indo-Pacific partners reflects both strategic necessity and political uncertainty.

item.title: In Trump’s world, companies seek insurance against political risk
item.description: It could soon get more expensive

item.title: An attack on the world economy
item.description: Whatever happens in the Strait of Hormuz, energy markets have been changed for ever

item.title: There are no good options for Iran’s nuclear programme
item.description: If America cannot eliminate the threat, what should it do?

item.title: The Irresistible Urge to Invoke World War III
item.description: Forget the inflated comparisons. Neither the war in the Middle East nor the Russian invasion of Ukraine ever signaled a global conflagration.

item.title: Consumers or Workers First?
item.description: A well-functioning economy, say most economists, is one that provides a widening array of ever-more affordable goods and services. But what we need is an economic-policy mindset that recognizes that people are both consumers and workers.

item.title: Inside Hong Kong’s Cage Homes: The Elderly Residents on the Frontlines of the Heat Crisis
item.description: As temperatures soar in the city, elderly people living alone in poorly ventilated homes are among those at greatest risk of worsening health conditions – and even death.

item.title: Laundering Dirty Crypto in Cambodia
item.description: A digital currency pegged to the U.S. dollar keeps cyber criminals afloat.

item.title: The Autonomous Battlefield
item.description: The U.S. military isn’t ready for it.

item.title: The Dangers of a Weak Iran
item.description: A wounded Islamic Republic can still threaten the world.

item.title: Thai-flagged Cargo Vessel Hit By Iranian Projectiles in Strait of Hormuz
item.description: The Royal Thai Navy says that three members of the 23-person crew remain unaccounted for after yesterday's attack.

item.title: Southeast Asia Reels From Middle East Oil Supply Shortages
item.description: From Manila to Hanoi, the region's governments are taking steps to absorb supply shocks and depress demand for increasingly scarce fuels.

item.title: The Prospect of Impeaching Philippine Vice President Sara Duterte
item.description: Duterte is accused of betraying the public trust by committing graft and corruption, bribery, and other high crimes.

item.title: Could special forces truly obliterate Iran’s nuclear programme?
item.description: Swiping the country’s uranium would require one of the largest raids in military history
